paragraphs.text:
SRD‑46 shows that **mercury complex chemistry is dominated by soft‑donor ligands**, especially for **Hg(II)**. The main inorganic mercury entries are:

| Metal | SRD-46 ID | Linked ligand count | Measurement count |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Hg(II) | `metal_71` | 310 | 1097 |
| Hg(I) | `metal_72` | 31 | 83 |

paragraphs.text:
## Big picture

For **Hg(II)**, the database overview is strongly consistent with the classic pattern:

| Ligand class seen in SRD-46 | Mercury(II) behavior |
|---|---|
| Halides | Important; chloride, bromide, and especially iodide are well represented |
| Cyanide / pseudohalides | Very important; cyanide stands out as a major high-stability system |
| Sulfur / selenium donors | Dominant and characteristic for Hg(II): thiourea, thiosulfate, sulfide/selenide-related ligands, thioglycolate, BAL, glutathione, etc. |
| Nitrogen donors | Present but generally secondary to soft S/Se donors |
| Oxygen-rich carboxylates / aminopolycarboxylates | Present, but less distinctive than soft-donor chemistry |

A useful shorthand from the retrieved data is:

| Broad affinity trend for Hg(II) | Comment |
|---|---|
| S/Se ≳ CN⁻ / I⁻ >> N > O | Heuristic only, but matches the SRD-46 results returned here |

paragraphs.text:
## Hg(II): what kinds of complexes are common?

The Hg(II) search returned a wide range of ligand systems. Particularly notable families were:

| Family | Examples seen in SRD-46 |
|---|---|
| Halomercury complexes | chloride, bromide, iodide |
| Cyanomercury complexes | HCN/CN⁻ |
| Hydroxo complexes | hydroxide-derived species |
| Sulfur donor complexes | thiourea, thiosemicarbazide, thioglycolate, thiosulfate, BAL, glutathione |
| Selenium donor complexes | selenourea, selenosemicarbazide, hydroselenide, selenocyanate |
| N-donor chelates | ethylenediamine, diethylenetriamine, tetraamines, pyridyl amines, phenanthroline |
| O/N/O chelates | acetate, aminopolycarboxylates, crown-like systems |

The Hg(II) result slice also indicated that **multiple stoichiometries** are reported for several systems, especially:

| Ligand type | Interpretation from retrieved records |
|---|---|
| Halides | Repeated β/logK entries consistent with stepwise chloro-, bromo-, and iodo-complex formation |
| Cyanide | Multiple entries consistent with very stable higher-ligand stoichiometries |
| Hydroxide | Hydrolysis / hydroxo-speciation is important |
| S/Se donor ligands | Multiple β values suggest several stoichiometries and/or protonation states |

paragraphs.text:
## Hg(I): much narrower coverage, but still informative

Hg(I) has much less coverage in SRD‑46 than Hg(II), but the available ligand space is still clear.

| Metal | SRD-46 ID | Linked ligand count | Measurement count |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Hg(I) | `metal_72` | 31 | 83 |

From the Hg(I) stability search, the **logK-covered ligand set** includes:

| Ligand examples for Hg(I) | Notes from SRD-46 summary |
|---|---|
| Acetate | Most represented ligand in the Hg(I) logK slice |
| Hydroxide | Multiple records |
| Hydrogen sulfate / phosphate / pyrophosphate / triphosphate | Several oxyanion systems |
| Chloride, bromide, iodide | Present, but with fewer records than Hg(II) |
| Thiocyanate | Present |
| Cyanide / HCN | Present, but sparse in this slice |
| Carbonate / bicarbonate | Present |
| Small carboxylates | formate, propionate, glycolate, succinate, oxalate, etc. |
| Aniline | A rare neutral N-donor example |
| Macrocyclic entries | Two macrocycle-like ligands with ΔS/ΔH/logK data |

The Hg(I) records in this result set are mostly around **25–27 °C** and span **ionic strengths from 0 to 3 M**, with several entries marked as involving **solid phases**.

paragraphs.text:
## Practical trends supported by the retrieved data

| Trend | Evidence from SRD-46 results |
|---|---|
| Hg(II) chemistry is far richer than Hg(I) | 310 ligands / 1097 measurements for Hg(II) vs 31 ligands / 83 measurements for Hg(I) |
| Soft ligands dominate Hg(II) coordination | Strong representation of sulfur-, selenium-, cyanide-, and iodide-based systems |
| Halide chemistry is central | Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻ recur prominently, with iodide especially notable |
| Mercury speciation is condition-sensitive | Returned records span different ionic strengths, temperatures, and sometimes solid/non-aqueous contexts |
| Hydrolysis matters | Hydroxide appears for both Hg(II) and Hg(I), indicating hydroxo chemistry is important |

paragraphs.text:
## Chemistry insight

Mercury’s SRD‑46 profile is exactly what HSAB theory predicts: **Hg(II) behaves as a soft Lewis acid**, so it binds especially strongly to **soft bases** like sulfur, selenium, iodide, and cyanide. That is why biological thiols, sulfur chelators, and halide-rich media can dramatically change mercury mobility and speciation.

If you want, I can next give you a **table of specific mercury stability constants** from SRD‑46 for one class, such as **Hg–chloride**, **Hg–cyanide**, **Hg–thiosulfate**, or **Hg–EDTA**.
